risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "An author (W.R.M.) generated the randomization sequence. Using an exact 1:1 treatment distribution, we initially created 50 random assignments. We then used the first 30 assignments to create a nearly equal 1:1 distribution to both treatment strategies" Comment: The study explicitly reports a randomization sequence was generated, with random assignments created in advance. Although the exact algorithm is not named, this is sufficient context to indicate a proper random allocation sequence rather than a non-random rule.

Quote: "After obtaining written informed consent, a study coordinator opened an opaque envelope with the preassigned treatment, either appendectomy with perioperative antibiotics or antibiotics alone (with subsequent appendectomy, if necessary)." Comment: The paper explicitly states that assignment was placed in an opaque envelope and opened only after consent/enrollment, which indicates a mechanism preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment. Although the authors do not say the envelopes were sequentially numbered and sealed, the direct description of an opaque envelope containing the preassigned treatment supports low risk for allocation concealment under the trial report.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Because a sham surgery would be unethical, these investigations are unblinded and management of participants and outcome assessments could be biased." Comment: Participants and treating personnel were not blinded to assignment (antibiotics-first vs urgent appendectomy), and the study explicitly states the trial was unblinded. Knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence several outcomes assessed in this trial, including pain, analgesic use, activity, quality of life, and hospital discharge timing/management, so the dominant risk for this domain is high.

Quote: "because a sham surgery would be unethical, these investigations are unblinded and management of participants and outcome assessments could be biased." Comment: Outcome assessment was not blinded, and many key outcomes were patient-reported or judgment-dependent (pain, analgesic use, activity, quality of life, symptom resolution, discharge/return-to-normal-activity assessments), so knowledge of allocation could influence measurement. Although some outcomes (e.g. laboratory values, CT findings, major complications) are more objective, the study explicitly acknowledges unblinded outcome assessment and the main patient-centered outcomes were susceptible to detection bias.

Quote: "At follow-up, all participants completed therapy in their assigned group and all except one (96.7%) were seen or contacted at each specified follow-up visit. One participant in the antibiotics-first group had a follow-up telephone call at day four and reported complete recovery but did not return subsequently." Comment: Attrition was minimal and very well balanced overall, with only 1 of 30 participants missing some later follow-up and no indication that the missing data were related to poor outcome or differed by group in a way likely to bias results. The paper also reports near-complete follow-up at all specified visits, which supports a Low risk judgement for incomplete outcome data.
